title: The duty is the overlay, not a makeweight
paras: The duty is the overlay, not a makeweight. Heritage decisions sit under statutory duties, and those duties aren't ordinary material considerations to be freely weighed. Understanding their status is the foundation of every heritage case. Section 66(1) of the Planning, Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas Act 1990 requires the decision-maker, when considering whether to grant permission for development that affects a listed building or its setting, to have special regard to the desirability of preserving the building, its setting, and any features of special architectural or historic interest it possesses. Section 72(1) requires that, in the exercise of planning functions in a conservation area, special attention is paid to the desirability of preserving or enhancing the character or appearance of that area. These duties overlay the whole decision. And the courts treat them as carrying considerable importance and weight — not as one factor among many to be traded away.

Now, the word “preserving” is narrower than it looks, and its meaning matters. In South Lakeland District Council against the Secretary of State — reported at 1992, U-K-H-L 17, and at 1992, 2 A-C 141 — the House of Lords held that to preserve means to do no harm. A scheme that leaves the building, its setting or the character of the area unharmed satisfies the duty, even if it adds nothing. But a scheme that harms them does not. The duty is negative in form — it doesn't compel positive enhancement — but it bites hard against harm. And because the conservation-area duty is expressed in the alternative, to preserve or enhance, a scheme that does neither can fail it where the context called for one or the other.

The weight the duty carries was settled in Barnwell Manor Wind Energy Limited against East Northamptonshire District Council, reported at 2014, E-W-C-A Civ 137. The Court of Appeal held that section 66 requires the decision-maker to give considerable importance and weight to the desirability of preserving the setting of a listed building — and that this creates a strong statutory presumption against granting permission for development that would harm it. Crucially, that presumption applies even where the harm is characterised as less than substantial. A finding of limited harm does not license a level, neutral balance. Forge Field — that's R on the application of the Forge Field Society against Sevenoaks District Council, reported at 2014, E-W-H-C 1895, Admin — drove the point home. The presumption is strong, and any public benefit relied on to outweigh the harm must be of commensurate force. In practice, the decision-maker can't simply note the harm and move on. The harm must be given its statutory weight before any balancing begins.

The duty and the Framework's heritage policy are related, but they're not the same thing. In Jones against Mordue, reported at 2015, E-W-C-A Civ 1243, the Court of Appeal indicated that a decision-maker who works through the Framework's heritage paragraphs properly will generally also have discharged the section 66 duty — the so-called Mordue shortcut. But the shortcut is a presumption of compliance, not a substitute. The duty is not subsumed by the policy. A decision that applies the policy mechanically while forgetting the statutory duty, or that treats heritage harm as a neutral makeweight, is vulnerable to challenge as an error of law. The safe discipline is this: identify the duty expressly, apply the strong presumption, and only then turn to the policy balance.

Scheduled monuments sit in a parallel regime. Works to a scheduled monument require scheduled monument consent under the Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Areas Act 1979 — a separate consent from listed building consent and from planning permission, and one the local authority does not grant. But the significance and setting of a scheduled monument, just like those of a listed building, remain material to any planning decision that affects them. And the structural lesson generalises: identify every designation in play, and the regime that governs each, before you assess harm.

title: Significance is the whole game
paras: Significance is the whole game. Significance is the value of a heritage asset to this and future generations because of its heritage interest — and it derives not only from the asset's fabric but also from its setting. Everything in a heritage assessment turns on it. You can't assess harm to a significance you haven't established, and a decision or a Heritage Statement that hasn't first pinned down what the significance is hasn't started. The recurring failing is to describe what an asset is — its date, its materials, its listing grade — rather than why it matters.

The Framework identifies four interests through which significance is understood: archaeological, architectural, artistic and historic. Architectural interest lies in design, craftsmanship and the quality of the building as a piece of architecture. Historic interest lies in the illustrative or associative links an asset has to past people, events and aspects of life. Artistic interest lies in design or decorative quality. And archaeological interest lies in the potential of the fabric, or the ground, to yield evidence about the past. An asset will usually carry more than one, and they can pull in different directions — a plain building of high historic interest may have little architectural quality, and the assessment must say so, rather than flattening everything into a single verdict.

Practitioners also draw on the parallel values framework in Historic England's Conservation Principles, commonly summarised as evidential, historical, aesthetic and communal value. The two frameworks map onto one another, and both serve the same purpose — to force the question of what actually carries the significance, element by element. Conservation Principles is under review, so its status should be checked. But the analytical habit it teaches is the point. Significance is rarely spread evenly across an asset, and identifying which elements, spaces and features carry it — and which are neutral or intrusive — is what makes the later harm assessment defensible.

Assessment must be proportionate to the asset's importance, and no more than sufficient to understand the potential impact of the proposal on that significance. A grade one church and its designed setting warrant a fuller statement than a modest unlisted building in a conservation area, and the effort should be directed at the significance the proposal actually engages — there's little value in a page of general history, and none on the specific fabric to be altered. A thin statement for an important asset, and bloated boilerplate for a modest one, are equal and opposite failures. The output is a Statement of Significance that identifies the elements carrying significance and the contribution of setting, prepared in line with Historic England's advice. And that's the document the whole assessment then stands on.

title: The list description is not the significance
paras: The list description is not the significance. A common and costly trap is to treat the National Heritage List entry as if it defined an asset's significance. It doesn't — and the distinction is one inspectors return to repeatedly. The entry describes the asset and explains, often briefly, why it was listed. It's a finding aid, not a definition. Listing protects the whole building — exterior and interior — and can extend beyond it, so the significance an assessment must grapple with is frequently far wider than the words of the entry suggest.

The extent of listing is set by section 1(5) of the 1990 Act. Listing covers the entire building, inside and out. It extends to any object or structure fixed to the building. And it extends to any object or structure within the curtilage of the building which, although not fixed, has formed part of the land since before the 1st of July 1948. So the significance of an interior the entry doesn't mention, or of a curtilage barn, wall, gatepier or outbuilding, may be substantial and fully protected — even though the list description is silent about it. Getting the extent of listing wrong, assuming an interior or a curtilage structure is unprotected, is a frequent and serious error. Cross-refer the lawful development and curtilage material for the tests.

Establish significance from the entry, and well beyond it. Use the Historic Environment Record. Use historic Ordnance Survey and tithe maps that show how the building and its plot have changed. Use archives, photographs and any earlier heritage assessments. And above all, inspect inside and out — because much of what carries significance, a historic plan form, a roof structure, surviving joinery or plaster, is invisible from the street and absent from the entry. Inspectors treat the list description as a starting point and expect the applicant to have looked past it. An assessment that relies on the entry as the definition of significance is routinely given little weight.

title: Setting is not just the view
paras: Setting is not just the view. Setting is the surroundings in which a heritage asset is experienced. Its extent isn't fixed, and — this is the point practitioners most often miss — it isn't only visual. Historic, functional, designed and experiential relationships can all contribute to the significance an asset derives from its setting. So land can matter to an asset even where there's no direct view between the two, and land within sight of an asset may contribute nothing at all. The question is never simply whether the asset can be seen. It's whether, and how, the surroundings contribute to its significance.

In Catesby Estates Limited against Steer, reported at 2018, E-W-C-A Civ 1697, the Court of Appeal confirmed that setting is not limited to visual or physical connection. Historic, social and economic links can contribute to the significance an asset derives from its setting. But the court also cautioned that a merely remote or conceptual connection may contribute little or nothing — so setting is not boundless. The exercise is to identify the real contribution, not to draw the widest possible circle. And practical judgement is needed on both sides. An over-tight setting that stops at the boundary misses a genuine contribution, while an over-generous one that sweeps in unrelated land invites the response that the link is too tenuous to matter.

Assess setting through Historic England's staged approach in Good Practice Advice 3. First, identify the heritage assets affected, and their settings. Second, assess whether, and how, the setting contributes to the significance of each asset — considering the experience of the asset in the round, including approaches, key views, the relationship between the asset and its surroundings, and how that relationship is understood as one moves through the landscape. Third, assess the effect of the proposal on that contribution. Fourth, explore ways to maximise enhancement and to avoid or minimise harm. And fifth, make and document the decision. Inspectors expect to see this reasoning worked through — not a bare assertion that a scheme is or isn't visible. The guidance edition should be checked, but the discipline of the staged approach is settled practice.

title: Harm is to significance, not to bricks
paras: Harm is to significance, not to bricks. Harm in heritage terms is harm to significance — not damage to fabric as such. Works to fabric, development within a setting, the loss of an element that contributes to significance, or incremental and cumulative change can all harm significance. And it's the effect on significance that must be assessed and located, not the physical intervention counted. A large intervention in an area of low significance may cause little harm; a small intervention in the most significant fabric may cause a great deal. The categories the policy then applies are three: no harm; less than substantial harm; and substantial harm or total loss.

Substantial harm is a high threshold, and this is the single most litigated point in the field. In Bedford Borough Council against the Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government, reported at 2012, E-W-H-C 4344, Admin, the court held that substantial harm means an impact so serious that very much, if not all, of the significance of the asset is drained away, or vitiated. It's not merely “considerable” harm, and it isn't reached simply because an important asset is affected. Total demolition of a listed building, or works that gut its historic interest, will be substantial harm or total loss. A well-designed extension, or development in part of a wider setting, will rarely cross the line. The practical consequence is that most real-world heritage harm — including much that is genuinely serious — falls into the less than substantial band. And that's why the correct handling of that band matters so much.

Keep the assessment honest by locating the harm precisely, rather than reaching for a label. Say whether the effect is no harm; less than substantial — and, within that band, whether it sits at the lower, middle or upper end; or substantial. The three categories carry very different policy consequences, so both inflating ordinary harm to “substantial” to strengthen a refusal, and deflating real harm below the line to ease a grant, are errors that unravel on scrutiny. And assess setting and cumulative effects alongside direct works to fabric. In a conservation area in particular, the incremental loss of significance across many small approvals is a real and recognised form of harm — not a reason to treat each application in isolation.

title: “Less than substantial” is not “less than substantial weight”
paras: “Less than substantial” is not “less than substantial weight.” The single most misunderstood point in heritage practice is that less than substantial harm does not mean less than substantial weight. The phrase describes which policy test applies to the harm. It says nothing about how much the harm weighs in that test. A finding of less than substantial harm still attracts great weight under policy, and considerable importance and weight under the statutory duty. Treating the label as a signal that the harm is minor is the error that loses more heritage appeals than any other.

Policy requires great weight to be given to the conservation of a designated heritage asset. And — this is the part that's routinely dropped — the more important the asset, the greater that weight. Grade one and grade two star listed buildings, scheduled monuments, protected wreck sites, registered battlefields, grade one and grade two star registered parks and gardens, and World Heritage Sites are all identified as assets of the highest significance. Harm to them carries correspondingly greater weight. The great-weight requirement applies whether the harm is substantial or less than substantial; it is not switched off by the harm falling into the lower category. And any harm to, or loss of, the significance of a designated asset requires clear and convincing justification — a demanding standard that a general appeal to benefit does not meet.

The courts have shut down the “less than substantial weight” fallacy directly. In City and Country Bramshill Limited against the Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government, reported at 2021, E-W-C-A Civ 320, the Court of Appeal confirmed that the categorisation of harm, and the heritage balance, are matters of planning judgement. But it held that less than substantial harm is not to be equated with a less than substantial planning objection. The harm still attracts great weight, and the decision-maker must weigh it as such. The discipline that follows is to state, in terms, both the category of harm and the weight given to it — and to calibrate that weight to the importance of the asset, so a grade two building and a grade one building aren't treated alike.

title: Two balances, and how not to double-count
paras: Two balances, and how not to double-count. There are two balances in a heritage case, and confusing them is where assessments and decisions most often fail. The internal heritage balance is a policy test applied to the heritage harm alone — harm against the public benefits that bear on that harm. The external, overall balance then weighs the whole planning case, heritage included. Substantial harm and less than substantial harm run different internal tests. And because the heritage harm already carries great weight, the same harm, or the same benefit, must not be counted twice as it moves from the internal balance to the overall one.

For substantial harm or total loss, the internal test is strict. Permission should be refused unless it's demonstrated either that the harm is necessary to achieve substantial public benefits that outweigh it, or that a narrow set of alternative conditions is met in full. First, that the nature of the asset prevents all reasonable uses. Second, that no viable use can be found in the medium term through appropriate marketing. Third, that conservation by grant funding, or some form of not-for-profit ownership, is demonstrably not possible. And fourth, that the harm is outweighed by the benefit of bringing the site back into use. Such harm should be wholly exceptional for the highest-significance assets, and exceptional for others. For less than substantial harm, the test is different in structure: the harm is weighed against the public benefits of the proposal, including, where appropriate, securing the asset's optimum viable use. Choosing the wrong test — running the substantial-harm limbs on less than substantial harm, or the reverse — is a self-inflicted error.

Public benefits can be heritage benefits — securing the long-term conservation of the asset, or revealing or better presenting its significance — or wider benefits such as housing, including affordable housing, economic activity, environmental gains, regeneration and public access. But they must be genuine public benefits, not private gain to the applicant. They must flow from the proposal. And they must be deliverable, rather than speculative. Because the harm already carries great weight, a modest public benefit won't readily outweigh even less than substantial harm to an important asset. Two further concepts recur. Optimum viable use is the use that causes the least harm to significance while keeping the asset in viable use — not necessarily the most profitable use. Securing it is itself a public benefit, while failing to secure any viable use can threaten the asset. And enabling development is development that would ordinarily be refused, permitted because it secures the future of an asset whose conservation can't otherwise be funded. It's governed by Historic England's tests, and their safeguards against abuse.

The heritage conclusion then feeds the overall planning balance, which is handed to the Decision-Making area of this app. The critical interaction is this. Designated-heritage policy is among the protections capable of providing a clear reason for refusal, and where it does, it disengages the tilted balance. So a scheme that would otherwise benefit from the presumption in favour of sustainable development — because the authority can't demonstrate a five-year housing land supply — can still be refused on heritage grounds. City and Country Bramshill, reported at 2021, E-W-C-A Civ 320, addresses how the internal heritage balance and the overall balance relate. And the discipline it points to is to run each balance once, on its own terms — and never to count the same harm or the same benefit in both.

title: Archaeology, and why recording is not a cure
paras: Archaeology, and why recording is not a cure. Where a site has archaeological potential, the significance of any remains must be understood before the application is determined — through desk-based assessment and, where necessary, field evaluation such as geophysical survey or trial trenching — so that the decision is informed rather than deferred. And recording is not mitigation. It offsets, in part, the loss of knowledge that would otherwise be destroyed with the remains. But it does nothing for the loss of significance itself, and a Statement that presents recording as if it neutralised harm should not be believed.

The order of operations is the point that inspectors and the courts hold firm on. The Framework requires, where a site has or may have archaeological interest, an appropriate desk-based assessment — and, where the desk-based assessment is insufficient, field evaluation — so that significance is understood before determination. Deferring that assessment to a post-permission condition puts the decision the wrong way round. The decision-maker can't weigh harm to a significance that hasn't been established, and a condition requiring evaluation “to inform the development” is no substitute for evaluation that informs the decision whether to permit it at all.

Preservation in situ is the preferred outcome for archaeological remains of significance. Where harm to, or loss of, significance is nonetheless unavoidable and justified, a programme of archaeological recording and analysis may be secured by condition. But the ability to record the evidence is not a factor in deciding whether the loss should be permitted. Recording offsets the loss of knowledge; it does not mitigate the loss of significance. Finally, watch the status of non-designated archaeology. Non-designated heritage assets are generally subject to a balanced judgement, having regard to the scale of harm and the significance of the asset. But non-designated archaeological remains of demonstrably equivalent significance to scheduled monuments are treated under a distinct and more protective approach — identify them as such, rather than defaulting to the ordinary non-designated balance.

title: The method, step 1: identify the assets
paras: The method, step one: identify the assets. The method that follows is a repeatable, six-step sequence for assessing any heritage proposal, and its discipline is the order. Each step gates the next, and you can't assess harm to a significance you haven't established. The doctrine above supplies the principles; these steps operationalise them, and the overall and tilted balances are handed to the Decision-Making area. The first step is to identify every heritage asset the proposal could affect.

List them all. Designated assets — listed buildings, with their grade; conservation areas; scheduled monuments; registered parks and gardens; registered battlefields; World Heritage Sites — and non-designated assets, whether on a local list or identified during the application. And critically, include assets affected only through their setting, which need not be on or adjacent to the site. Interrogate the datasets rather than relying on the red line: the National Heritage List for England, the local Historic Environment Record, the local list, and the constraints map. The output is a schedule of assets, each with its designation type and grade, and a note of whether it's affected directly, through its setting, or both. Then ask: what is on and adjacent to the site? What lies within the wider setting whose experience the proposal could change? And are there non-designated assets, or sites of archaeological potential, that no dataset flags?

title: The method, step 2: understand significance
paras: The method, step two: understand significance. For each asset identified, articulate why it matters: the heritage interest or interests — archaeological, architectural, artistic, historic — the relative contribution of different elements and fabric, and the contribution of setting. State it proportionately to the asset's importance, directing the effort at the significance the proposal actually engages. And go beyond the list description, using the Historic Environment Record, historic maps and archives, and inspection inside and out.

The output is a proportionate Statement of Significance that identifies the elements carrying significance and the contribution of setting — the document every later step relies on. The common error is to describe what the asset is rather than why it matters, to treat the list entry as the significance, and to be disproportionate in either direction. Ask: what is the grade or designation? What are the principal elements of significance, and which fabric or elements carry it? And does the setting contribute, and how — visually, historically, functionally, by design, or experientially?

title: The method, step 3: assess the effect
paras: The method, step three: assess the effect. Identify precisely where and how the proposal affects significance — works to fabric, development within the setting, the loss of a contributing element, or cumulative change. Then locate the harm: no harm; less than substantial, and where on that spectrum; or substantial or total loss. Assess setting and cumulative effects, not only direct works to fabric.

The output is a reasoned statement of the effect on significance, with the harm located on the spectrum, and setting and cumulative effects addressed. The common errors are these: assessing damage to fabric instead of to significance; letting the “less than substantial” label do the work without locating the harm on the band; and inflating to substantial — which is a high bar, remember Bedford — or deflating below the line. Ask: which elements of significance are affected, and how seriously? Is the harm to fabric, to setting, or through the loss of a contributing element? And where on the spectrum does it sit — and can you justify that placement?

title: The method, step 4: apply the weight
paras: The method, step four: apply the weight. Apply the weight as two distinct steps. Give great weight to the asset's conservation, scaled by importance, whether the harm is substantial or less than substantial. And separately, discharge the statutory duty by giving the harm considerable importance and weight, and applying the strong presumption under sections 66 and 72 — that's Barnwell, and Forge Field. Require clear and convincing justification for any harm.

The output is an explicit statement of the weight attaching to the harm, calibrated to the asset's importance, with the statutory duty addressed on its own terms. The common errors are these: treating less than substantial harm as low weight; omitting the statutory duty because the N-P-P-F was cited — remember Mordue; and giving a grade two asset the same weight as a grade one. Ask: how important is the asset? Have you given the harm great weight, and considerable importance and weight, as two distinct steps? And is there clear and convincing justification for the harm?

title: The method, step 5: run the correct balance
paras: The method, step five: run the correct balance. Choose the correct internal test. Substantial harm or total loss runs the strict test — refuse unless the harm is necessary for substantial public benefits that outweigh it, or all four alternative limbs are met. Less than substantial harm runs the weighing test — weigh the harm against the public benefits of the proposal, including securing the asset's optimum viable use. Identify genuine, public, deliverable benefits. Weigh them against harm that already carries great weight. And then feed the conclusion into the overall balance, without double-counting.

The output is a reasoned internal heritage balance and a clear hand-off to the overall balance, stating whether heritage provides a clear reason for refusal. The common errors are these: running the wrong internal test; inflating benefits, or counting private gain; double-counting the same harm or benefit across the internal and overall balances; and forgetting that heritage can provide a clear reason for refusal that disengages the tilt. Ask: is the harm substantial or less than substantial, and which test therefore applies? What are the genuine public benefits — are they deliverable, and does optimum viable use feature? And does heritage switch off the tilted balance for this proposal?

title: The method, step 6: avoid, reduce, document
paras: The method, step six: avoid, reduce, document. Return to design and mitigation, to avoid or minimise the harm and to maximise enhancement — the final step of the Good Practice Advice 3 approach. Identify what can properly be secured by condition or obligation, such as recording and analysis, method statements, materials and management. Then record the judgement clearly, and in reasoned sequence — because the discipline of writing it is the discipline of making it.

The output is a finalised, reasoned assessment: significance stated, harm located and weighed, the correct balance run, mitigation and conditions identified, and a conclusion a decision-maker can adopt and defend. The common errors are these: presenting recording as if it mitigated the loss of significance; using conditions to defer the significance assessment that should have preceded determination; and a conclusion that asserts an outcome without showing the reasoning. Ask: can the harm be avoided or reduced by design change? What is properly secured by condition or obligation — and is recording being wrongly presented as mitigation? And does the written reasoning show each step, so the conclusion is defensible?

title: Worked: extension to a grade II listed building
paras: Now a worked example: an extension to a grade two listed building. This worked assessment applies the six-step method — it's illustrative, not a real case. The scenario is a two-storey rear extension to a grade two, early-nineteenth-century farmhouse, removing a late-twentieth-century lean-to and forming a new opening through a historic rear wall, where the interior retains its historic plan form and principal joinery. The significance lies in the architectural and historic interest of a modest but intact vernacular farmhouse — the symmetrical principal elevation, historic fabric and roof form, and a legible historic plan. The lean-to is a neutral-to-negative later accretion, and the setting contributes modestly.

On the effect: the new opening removes a section of historic rear fabric and slightly erodes the legibility of the plan, and the extension must remain subservient to the host. Removing the lean-to is a heritage benefit. The harm is located as less than substantial, at the lower-to-middle of the spectrum — real, but leaving the principal elevation, roof form and plan legible. On weight: great weight to conservation, considerable importance and weight and the strong statutory presumption under section 66, and clear and convincing justification required.

The balance runs the less-than-substantial test. The public benefits are modest but genuine — securing the continued optimum viable use of the house for modern family living, and the heritage gain of removing the unsympathetic lean-to. Weighed against harm carrying great weight, the outcome is finely poised, and it turns on design. If the opening is minimised, placed in less significant fabric and made reversible, and the materials match, the benefits are capable of outweighing the reduced harm. The conclusion is less than substantial harm capable of being outweighed on conditions — matching materials, joinery details, a reversible opening, and the lean-to removal secured. That's a defensible conditional grant, where a larger or more damaging opening would tip it to refusal.

title: Worked: development in a listed building’s setting
paras: A second worked example: development in a listed building's setting. The scenario is nine dwellings in an open field adjoining a grade two star medieval parish church, whose isolated, landmark rural setting contributes strongly to its significance. The significance is high — the architectural and historic interest of a grade two star medieval church — and the setting contributes strongly, because the church is experienced as a landmark across open fields on the approach to the village. A historic church-and-settlement relationship.

Applying the staged approach to the effect: the asset is identified; its setting plainly contributes; and the development would enclose the open foreground and erode both the church's landmark quality and the church-and-countryside relationship, from key views and approaches. The harm is located as less than substantial, but toward the upper end — because the affected asset is of the highest significance. On weight: great weight, elevated by the grade two star status — the more important the asset, the greater the weight — considerable importance and weight under section 66, and clear and convincing justification.

The balance runs the less-than-substantial test. The public benefit is a modest housing contribution, including affordable homes. Weighed against upper-end harm to a highest-significance asset carrying great weight, a modest housing benefit does not clearly outweigh it. And designated-heritage policy provides a clear reason for refusal that disengages the tilted balance — even if the authority's housing land supply is short. The conclusion is that refusal is the defensible outcome. The housing benefit doesn't outweigh the heritage harm to a grade two star asset, and heritage switches off the tilt. A clearer case than the finely poised extension.

title: Worked: loss in a conservation area
paras: A third worked example: loss in a conservation area. The scenario is demolition of an unlisted but locally distinctive Victorian villa that makes a positive contribution to a conservation area, and its replacement with a larger apartment building — in a street that has already seen incremental change. The significance in issue is the character and appearance of the conservation area under section 72. The villa is a positive-contribution building whose scale, materials and rhythm reinforce the area's Victorian character, and its loss is assessed as harm to the area.

On the effect: the loss of a positive-contribution building erodes the area's character; the replacement's scale and design are material; and there's a cumulative dimension — the incremental erosion sometimes called death by a thousand cuts. The harm is located as less than substantial. The loss of a single building rarely drains very much, if not all, of an area's significance — that's the Bedford high bar. But the harm is real, and it's sharpened by the cumulative context. On weight: great weight to the area's conservation, the section 72 duty of special attention, and the reminder that preserving means doing no harm — South Lakeland. A harmful scheme does not preserve.

The balance runs the less-than-substantial test, informed by the policy that the loss of a positive-contribution element is treated as substantial or less than substantial harm, and assessed accordingly. The public benefits are additional housing and — if the villa is genuinely incapable of viable retention — securing a viable use of the site. And this is where the case turns. The evidential burden is on the applicant to show that retention is not viable, and that the replacement would preserve or enhance the area. On robust viability evidence and a genuinely high-quality replacement, the benefits may tip it. On weak evidence, the loss, plus the cumulative erosion, outweighs a general housing benefit. The conclusion is finely balanced and evidence-dependent — a reasoned refusal where retention is viable and the replacement is unremarkable, and a possible conditional grant where unviability is robustly demonstrated and the replacement genuinely enhances the area.

title: What appeals consistently show
paras: Finally — what appeals consistently show. These are distilled patterns from Planning Inspectorate casework, expressed as practice rather than law, and without individual reference numbers. They're a fair generalisation of how inspectors approach significance, harm and public benefit — and they line up closely with the doctrine above.

Significance first. Inspectors expect significance to be established clearly before harm. An assessment that jumps to harm without first articulating significance is given little weight. And “less than substantial” is not trivial. It's routinely given great weight and considerable importance and weight, and inspectors typically locate the harm on the spectrum rather than letting the label carry the argument.

Public benefits are scrutinised. They must be genuine, public and deliverable, and they're weighed against harm that already carries great weight — so modest benefits rarely outweigh harm to an important asset. Setting harm is frequently decisive, and the staged Good Practice Advice 3 approach is expected, rather than a bare visual analysis. And the list description is treated as a starting point — not the definition of significance.

Cumulative erosion of conservation-area character is taken seriously where it's evidenced — the “thousand cuts” argument carries real weight. And recording is not accepted as mitigation for the loss of significance. Taken together, the pattern is this: inspectors reward rigour — significance established, harm located and weighed at its proper weight, benefits tested — and they're unimpressed by labels, assertion, and benefit claimed but not delivered.
